title: Getting 401 Unauthorized
body: Check your PANDASTACK_TOKEN is set and valid. Get a new token from dashboard settings.

title: Getting 402 Payment Required
body: This feature requires a paid plan. Upgrade to Pro, Team, or Enterprise.

title: Getting 429 Too Many Requests
body: You've hit the hourly sandbox creation limit. Wait or upgrade your plan.

title: Getting 503 Service Unavailable - no agents
body: No agents available. Contact support if this persists.

title: Getting 507 Insufficient Storage
body: Agent disk full. Volume provisioning failed — contact support or delete unused volumes.

title: Sandbox not responding or stuck
body: Check sandbox logs. Try exec with shorter timeout or restart the sandbox.

title: Database connection refused
body: Ensure TLS is enabled. Check connection_url from GET /v1/databases/{id}.

title: App deploy failing or stuck
body: Check deployment logs for errors. Verify framework detection and build commands.

title: Can't create sandbox - quota exceeded
body: You've reached your workspace sandbox limit. Delete old sandboxes or upgrade.

title: Using sandbox for machine learning / AI
body: Use code-interpreter template with Python. Install packages via exec or custom template.

title: Databases
body: Beta. Managed Databases are in public beta. The API is stable for create / list / get / delete, but advanced features (branching, read replicas, point-in-time restore) are still in progress — see Coming soon. Don't yet rely on a single beta database as the only copy of irreplaceable production data. A database is a managed PostgreSQL 16 instance running in its own dedicated Firecracker microVM — not a shared schema on a multi-tenant cluster. Each database gets its own kernel, its own process, a

title: Events
body: Every meaningful state change a sandbox goes through is published as a JSON event with a stable schema. You can either snapshot the recent history or subscribe to the live tail. Event schema - — server timestamp (millisecond precision, UTC). - — string from the catalog below. - — / / . Driven by the event type. - — type-specific structured fields (often duplicates of what's in ). title: Exec — running commands
body: is the bread-and-butter call on a PandaStack sandbox: send a command, get back stdout, stderr, an exit code, and how long it took. Everything else (notebooks, code interpreters, agent tools) is built on top. title: Filesystem
body: Every sandbox has a writable rootfs, scoped to its lifetime (or longer if you use volumes). The filesystem API lets you push files in and pull files out without going through + base64 dance. All four calls live under . There are no top-level / helpers. Quickstart Fetch the bytes at in the guest. The whole file is loaded into memory — for large files, stream via ( ) instead. | SDK | Returns | | ------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | | Python | . title: Logs
body: Every sandbox has a single combined console log (the Firecracker serial port + the in-guest agent's stdout/stderr). It's append-only, persists for the sandbox's lifetime, and is exposed two ways: snapshot or follow. Snapshot — read what's been logged so far Plain body, newline-delimited. returns the last N lines (default: full log). Omit and the response closes as soon as the file has been read — useful for -style polling. Follow — server-sent events Returns ; each frame is one log line. SDK equ

title: LSP
body: LSP proxies a Language Server Protocol server running inside a sandbox out to your editor or browser. You get autocomplete, hover, go-to-definition, diagnostics — all backed by the real toolchain in the sandbox, not a stub. This is what makes the in-dashboard editor "feel like VS Code" without us re-implementing a language server in the browser. Wire protocol Once upgraded, the WebSocket transports raw LSP framing in both directions: - client → server : standard LSP envelopes ( ). Binary or text

title: Hosted MCP Server
body: PandaStack ships a hosted, workspace-level MCP server . Where the per-sandbox MCP endpoint turns one sandbox into a tool, this endpoint turns your whole workspace into one: the model can create and destroy sandboxes, run commands, read/write files, provision Postgres databases, and trigger app deploys — all through a single URL and your existing API token. is an alias. The transport is stateless streamable-HTTP JSON-RPC 2.0 — every request stands alone, no session handshake required, so it works

title: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
body: PandaStack speaks MCP natively. Every sandbox is a fully-formed MCP server — point Claude Desktop, Cursor, the CLI, or your own agent at it and the model gets , , , and tools wired to a real Linux microVM. Looking for workspace-level tools (create/delete sandboxes, databases, app deploys) behind a single URL? That's the hosted MCP server at — this page covers the per-sandbox endpoint. Body is JSON-RPC 2.0. Stateless — every request stands alone, no session affinity needed. Methods Returns capabi

title: Networking — NATID mode
body: PandaStack uses a network mode called NATID (NAT-identity-preserving) to deliver fast snapshot restores. Understanding it matters when you're debugging connectivity. The problem Firecracker snapshots include the kernel's network state — interface IPs, ARP tables, DHCP leases. If we restore a snapshot into a VM that has a different IP than the one baked into the snapshot, the guest networking is broken until DHCP renegotiates. For 180 ms cold boots, we can't afford that. The solution Every snapsh

title: Observability
body: PandaStack ships first-class observability — every sandbox emits process-level metrics and lifecycle events that flow into ClickHouse, then back out through the dashboard as charts. No Grafana, no Prometheus to operate; it's built in. What gets collected Five tables in ClickHouse, all partitioned by workspace and retained per the SLA below. | Table | Cardinality | Retention | What's in it | | -------------------------- | ----------------- | --------- | -------------------------------------------

title: Performance
body: PandaStack's whole point is "start a real VM faster than you can blink." This page is the honest breakdown — what we measure, how the snapshot-restore boot path works, and what numbers you should plan against. title: Preview URLs
body: A preview URL is a public hostname that proxies HTTP(S) into a port inside your sandbox. It's how you let a teammate poke at a server, share a Streamlit app with a stakeholder, or hand a webhook URL to a third-party service — without exposing your API token. title: Sandbox lifecycle
body: A sandbox is a single Firecracker microVM with a dedicated kernel, rootfs, network namespace, and lifecycle. Sandboxes are identified by a UUID (e.g. ) and always belong to a workspace. States Lifetime guarantees | Transition | P50 | P99 | Notes | | ------------------ | -------- | -------- | ----- | | Create → running | 179 ms | 203 ms | Snapshot-natid path on every create, n=50 prod. See Performance. | | Pause → resume | <5 ms | 8 ms | Just / . No memory writes. | | Hibernate → wake | 150 ms

title: Snapshots and forks
body: Snapshots capture a sandbox state. Forks create a new sandbox from an existing sandbox. The supported API works from a live sandbox ID; SDK helpers that construct directly from a snapshot are not available today. CLI Python TypeScript API

title: Templates
body: A template is a snapshot-ready root filesystem with a pinned kernel. When you , the scheduler restores a snapshot of that template into a fresh microVM — that's how cold-start lands at 179 ms instead of "boot a kernel from scratch". Templates are immutable and content-addressed: once a snapshot exists for , every sandbox boots from the same bytes. Official catalog PandaStack ships 5 production templates. All names are stable and accepted by in the SDKs and in the CLI. CPU and memory are baked in

title: Terminal
body: The terminal endpoint hands you a real Linux PTY inside the sandbox over a WebSocket. It's what powers the dashboard's "Connect" tab, and it's the right primitive to embed if you're building an IDE or a CI live-tail. The endpoint upgrades an HTTP to a WebSocket. Opening a PTY Wire protocol after the upgrade: | Direction | Frame | Meaning | | --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- | | client → server | binary frame | Bytes

title: Volumes
body: A volume is a named ext4 image that lives on the host independently of any sandbox. Attach it to one or many sandboxes at create-time and mount it inside the guest like any other block device. Detach when the sandbox dies — the data stays. Lifecycle Backing layout: one file per volume at . Maximum size 65,536 MB (64 GiB) per volume on every plan. Names are workspace-scoped. Attach to a sandbox Each entry adds a device to the VM. Volumes appear in the guest in attach-order: | Attach position | Gu

title: Deploy on-prem
body: On-prem deployments run the PandaStack API, dashboard, scheduler, and one or more sandbox agents on your hardware. The CLI does not deploy clusters; use your infrastructure tooling to run the services, then point clients at the API. Domains For hosted PandaStack: - Marketing: - Dashboard: - API: - Docs: For an on-prem deployment, expose your own HTTPS API origin and set: Replace the API URL with your private origin when self-hosting. title: Functions overview
body: What are Functions? PandaStack Functions run your Python or Node.js code inside isolated Firecracker microVMs. Every invocation gets a fresh runtime — no shared state, no noisy neighbours, no cold-start containers leaking secrets between customers. Unlike AWS Lambda, PandaStack Functions: - Accept full directory bundles (not just single files) — ship your entire project with dependencies - Store code privately in GCS, never in your database - Emit golden metrics (p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate,

title: Schedules
body: What are Schedules? Schedules attach a cron expression to a function and trigger it automatically. Every scheduled run executes the target function in a fresh isolated microVM, just like a manual invocation. Create a schedule First deploy a function, then attach a schedule to it: The schedule stores: - - - - - - - Cron syntax Schedules use standard 5-field cron expressions: Examples: - — every 5 minutes - — every hour - — every day at 09:00 UTC - — every Monday at midnight UTC Pause and resume P

title: Self-host overview
body: PandaStack is split into a control plane, agents, and Firecracker-based sandbox hosts. Self-hosting means running those services yourself and pointing clients at your API endpoint. Client configuration Use the same SDKs and CLI against your endpoint: For a private deployment, replace with your HTTPS API origin. Build the CLI Smoke test Related deployment guides - Deploy on-prem - Deploy on Mac (M1/M2/M3) The CLI does not include subcommands; deployment automation lives outside the command surfac

title: AI agents
body: The most common reason people use PandaStack: their LLM agent needs to execute code or mutate a filesystem as part of its plan. Putting that inside a Firecracker microVM means a misbehaving model can't your laptop or exfiltrate creds. Pattern: one sandbox per agent turn Pattern: persistent agent workspace Persistent volumes are not exposed in the current SDKs. For now, keep agent state in the sandbox filesystem while the sandbox is alive, or upload and download the workspace between turns. Track

title: CI runners
body: PandaStack can run each CI job in a short-lived sandbox. Use tokens in CI secrets and clean up each sandbox at the end of the job. Environment Shell runner pattern TypeScript runner pattern Useful lifecycle controls

title: Claude Managed Agents
body: Claude Managed Agents let Anthropic run the agent loop — the model, the reasoning, the multi-turn session state — while the agent's tool calls execute in infrastructure you control . Anthropic calls this a self-hosted sandbox , and a PandaStack microVM is an ideal one: every session gets a fresh, isolated Firecracker VM that boots from a snapshot in 180 ms and can hibernate between turns. This guide wires the two together. By the end you'll have an agent whose , file, and code tool calls run ins

title: Code interpreter
body: The template runs Python inside a Firecracker microVM. Use for commands, for Python snippets, and for files. One-shot exec This spawns Python fresh each call. Variables don't persist between runs. Run Python code For shell snippets, use . File uploads For larger files, use upload and download helpers: Persistence Persistent volume APIs are not available yet. Keep work inside a live sandbox with a longer TTL, or download the workspace before cleanup. Track persistent storage work at GitHub issues

title: Data pipelines
body: Use disposable sandboxes to isolate ETL steps, scrapers, and batch transforms. Put files through and execute code with or . Python batch job TypeScript batch job CLI debugging

title: PandaStack
body: PandaStack Open-source Firecracker microVM sandboxes for AI agents and code execution. 60-second quickstart Create a sandbox from the local API: What it does - Firecracker microVM sandboxes with strong process and kernel isolation. - Snapshot anywhere and fork running environments instantly. - Sub-second boot on every create via baked snapshot restore. - Template-defined CPU, RAM, and disk quotas. - Network egress controls for safer code execution. - Template-based images: Docker images converte

title: Fork & Copy-on-Write
body: produces a new sandbox whose initial state is a byte-perfect copy of the parent — same processes, same open file descriptors, same -installed packages, same in-memory variables. We do it in 400–750 ms per child, in parallel. Two CoW layers make it possible. Layer 1: memory CoW (Firecracker snapshot) We don't actually duplicate the parent's RAM. We: 1. Pause the parent ( 5 ms). 2. Write the parent's memory to (this is a copy of the RAM out to disk — the only expensive step, 30 ms for 256 MiB to N

title: Networking
body: Every sandbox lives in its own Linux network namespace, with its own MAC and its own private IP. The host doesn't share an IP space with the guest, the guests don't share an IP space with each other, and yet every guest can reach the internet and be reached on whatever ports it exposes. This page is how. The per-sandbox netns For sandbox : Inside the netns lives one tap device ( ) that Firecracker plumbs into the VM's . The veth pair shuttles packets between the netns and the root namespace wher

title: Scheduler
body: PandaStack has a tiny global scheduler — a handful of SQL queries plus a deterministic scoring function — that picks the right agent host for every create. It's not Kubernetes; it doesn't need to be. The whole thing fits in one file. The decision Whichever agent scores highest wins the create. Agents that fail the resource floor (requested CPU/RAM doesn't fit in free capacity) are excluded before scoring. Every create takes the same snapshot-restore fast path on any agent that has the template's

title: Snapshot & Restore
body: PandaStack doesn't cold-boot Linux every time you ask for a sandbox. We boot Linux once at template-bake time, write the entire VM state (memory + CPU registers + device state) to disk, and on every subsequent create we that state into a fresh Firecracker process and let it execute the very next instruction. That's how p50 is 179 ms and p99 is 203 ms on bare-metal Intel Cascade Lake. This page walks the full path, end to end. The artifacts on disk For each template (e.g., ) we keep three files o

title: Streaming Restore (UFFD)
body: Snapshot restore is fast because is already a local file: Firecracker s it and pages fault in lazily. But that assumes the file is on the host . On a cold host — one that has never restored this template — the agent first has to download the entire (256 MiB – several GiB) from object storage before Firecracker can it. That download is the "cold host first-boot cliff": the create blocks on transferring memory the guest may never even touch. Streaming restore removes that cliff. Instead of downloa

title: Architecture
body: PandaStack has three main layers: 1. Control plane — API, auth, organizations, tokens, templates, billing, and orchestration. 2. Data plane — agents that run sandbox operations on hosts. 3. Workload microVMs — Firecracker sandboxes created from templates. Public surfaces - Marketing: - Dashboard: - API: - Docs: API entry points Sandbox operations are scoped under . Filesystem access uses , lifecycle uses routes such as , , , , , and , and streaming uses , , and WebSocket upgrades. Agent-proxied

title: Supabase auth
body: Supabase auth for self-hosting Use Supabase Auth when your PandaStack deployment has more than one human user, more than one organization, or a dashboard exposed beyond a single trusted development machine. Local development can run in stub auth mode. Production-like self-hosting should use Supabase or another OIDC/JWT-compatible identity provider. When to use it Choose Supabase auth when you need: - dashboard sign-in for multiple users - organization membership and invitations - API requests ti
